coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on Resonance Rayleigh Scattering and inner filter effect for detecting alizarin red and Pb2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Sun, Zhonghui
Ma, Tengbiao
Sun, Xiaodong
Xu, Jinxia
Wang, Ruijie
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: A new ratiometric fluorescent strategy for detection of alizarin red (ARS) was designed based on the fluorescence of CDs and scattered light of scatterer. The CDs-ARS system can be used to detect Pb2+ based on that the complexation between ARS and Pb2+. With the addition of ARS, the fluorescence of CDs was apparently quenched via inner filter effect (IFE). Resonance Rayleigh Scattering (RRS) at 350 nm was enhanced by an increase in the number of scatterer. The value of ln(I350/I425) was linearly correlated with ARS concentration in the range of 0–80 μM, and the detection limit for ARS was calculated to be 68.1 nM. When Pb2+ was added to the CDs-ARS system, the complexation of ARS with Pb2+ increased the size of the scatterer, resulting in the increase of the RRS intensity at 350 nm. Due to the affinity between ARS and Pb2+, the overlap of the emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of ARS was reduced, resulting in the IFE effect was inhibited and the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs. The value of I350/I425 linearly increased with the addition of Pb2+ within the range of 10–50 μM, the limit of detection was 36.8 nM. As for practical application, CDs and CDs-ARS were applied to detect ARS and Pb2+ in tap water and poor water, respectively. The recovery values were obtained to be 95.4–98.8% and 93.4–101.7%. Furthermore, the system of CDs-ARS has been successfully applied to H1299 cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Alizarin red
Pb2+
Ratiometric
Inner filter effect
Resonance Rayleigh Scattering
Real water samples
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of CDs
Fluorescence responses of CDs for detection of ARS
Mechanism of CDs for detection of ARS
Detection of Pb2+ with the system of CDs-ARS
Fluorescence responses and selectivity of CDs-ARS for detection of Pb2+
Mechanism of CDs-ARS for detection of Pb2+
Determination of ARS and Pb2+ in real water sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
National Natural Science Foundation of China
Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CDs (inset) corresponding size distribution histogram of CDs; (b) FTIR spectra of CDs; (c) XPS analysis of CDs; (d)High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s; (e) High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s; (f) High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s.
(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum of CDs; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under varied excitation wavelengths.

(a) RRS intensity of 20 μM ARS with the time from 0 to 8 min; (b) RRS intensity of 50 μM ARS with the time from 0 to 8 min; (c) RRS intensity of 80 μM ARS with the time from 0 to 8 min; (d) RRS intensity of different concentration of ARS at 8 min.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at varied ARS concentrations (0–80 μM) under 350 nm excitation wavelength; (b) Linear relationship of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity ln(I350/I425) versus varied ARS concentrations (0–80 μM).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs + ARS, Pb2+, ARS and ARS + Pb2+; (b) UV-VIS absorption of ARS and the excitation and emission of CDs; (c) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs and CDs + ARS.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs + ARS(80 μM) at varied Pb2+ concentrations (0–50 μM) under 350 nm excitation wavelength; (b) Linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity ratio I350/I425 versus varied Pb2+ concentrations (10–50 μΜ); (c) The selectivity fluorescence spectra of CDs-ARS for Pb2+; (d) Competitive histogram of CDs-ARS for Pb2+.
The schematic illustration of CDs as probe for detecting ARS and the CDs-ARS system as probe for detecting Pb2+.
The estimated cell viability (%) by MTT assay.

(a) bright field confocal images of CDs in H1299 cells; (b) bright field confocal images of the CDs + ARS in H1299 cells; (c) bright field confocal images of the CDs + ARS + Pb2+ in H1299 cells; (d) black field confocal fluorescence images of CDs in H1299 cells; (e) black field confocal fluorescence images of the CDs + ARS in H1299 cells; (f) black field confocal fluorescence images of the CDs + ARS + Pb2+ in H1299 cells.
Parameters used to calculate IFE of ARS on the fluorescence of CDs.
Comparison of some reported methods with our method for Pb2+ detection.
The IFE mechanism of CDs-ARS with different concentrations of Pb2+.
Analytical results for the ARS detection of spiked in tap water and pool water samples.
Analytical results for the Pb2+ detection of spiked in tap water and pool water samples.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobes based on Resonance Rayleigh Scattering and inner filter effect for detecting alizarin red and Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes/National Center for International Joint Research on Separation Membranes, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Anesthesiology, The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Harbin Medical University, Harbin, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Anesthesiology, The Fourth Affiliated Hospital, Harbin Medical University, Harbin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new ratiometric fluorescent strategy for detection of alizarin red (ARS) was designed based on the fluorescence of CDs and scattered light of scatterer. The CDs-ARS system can be used to detect Pb2+ based on that the complexation between ARS and Pb2+. With the addition of ARS, the fluorescence of CDs was apparently quenched via inner filter effect (IFE). Resonance Rayleigh Scattering (RRS) at 350 nm was enhanced by an increase in the number of scatterer. The value of ln(I350/I425) was linearly correlated with ARS concentration in the range of 0–80 μM, and the detection limit for ARS was calculated to be 68.1 nM. When Pb2+ was added to the CDs-ARS system, the complexation of ARS with Pb2+ increased the size of the scatterer, resulting in the increase of the RRS intensity at 350 nm. Due to the affinity between ARS and Pb2+, the overlap of the emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of ARS was reduced, resulting in the IFE effect was inhibited and the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs. The value of I350/I425 linearly increased with the addition of Pb2+ within the range of 10–50 μM, the limit of detection was 36.8 nM. As for practical application, CDs and CDs-ARS were applied to detect ARS and Pb2+ in tap water and poor water, respectively. The recovery values were obtained to be 95.4–98.8% and 93.4–101.7%. Furthermore, the system of CDs-ARS has been successfully applied to H1299 cell imaging.

The schematic illustration of CDs as probe for detecting ARS and the CDs-ARS system as probe for detecting Pb2+.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as novel nanoparticles have drawn wide attentions of people. CDs show many merits including good water solubility, photostability and biocompatibility [1–3]. Because of these advantages of CDs, it had been widely applied in the field of detection [4,5]. Fluorescence quenching of CDs is often used in the detection process. The common quenching mechanisms include static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [6–8]. Though comparing the characteristic of the mechanisms, IFE avoids complicated modification process for CDs and has the advantage of simple design [9–12].

Resonance Rayleigh Scattering (RRS) has the advantages of simplicity and high sensitivity, enabling it to successfully detect trace analytes such as ions and biomacromolecules [13–18]. Generally, the intensity of RRS is related to the size and number of scatterer in the solution [19–24]. Our team has used CDs and other fluorescent substances such as curcumin to form a sensing platform for the detection of analytes [25]. In general, conventional ratiometric fluorescence systems are designed using fluorescent materials of two different emission wavelengths, but finding fluorescent materials with ratiometric probe characteristics remains a challenge. Therefore, it is necessary to establish a simple ratiometric method to reduce the interference of environmental and background factors, and ultimately improve the sensing accuracy [26–30]. However, few researches have been reported on using the fluorescence of CDs and RRS to design and construct ratiometric probe. Alizarin red (ARS), as an anthraquinone compound, is one of the common organic pollutants in sewage due to its high toxicity and complex structure [31–33]. Pb2+ is very easily accumulated in human nervous systems when exposed to air and water sources owing to its non-biodegradability [34,35]. It is well known that ARS can react with Pb2+ to form complexes. Based on the mechanism of IFE and RRS, we designed rapid and sensitive methods for the detection of ARS and Pb2+ at trace level [36–39].

Herein, CDs was used to design the ratiometric nanoprobe for detecting ARS based on IFE and RRS. Moreover, the CDs-ARS system be used to detect Pb2+ based on that ARS can be combined with Pb2+. When ARS was added, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by ARS through IFE and the RRS of scatterers was enhanced. Based on this principle, CDs were used as ratiometric probe for detecting ARS. When adding Pb2+ in the CDs-ARS system, ARS can complex with Pb2+, which leaded to the decline of overlap between the emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of ARS. It inhibited the process of IFE, which leaded to the fluorescence recovery of CDs. Meanwhile, the formation of complexes enhanced RRS. Ratiometric detecting of Pb2+ can be achieved via the CDs-ARS system. In addition, CDs and CDs-ARS were applied for the detection of ARS and Pb2+ in real water samples, as well as for H1299 cell imaging, which demonstrated the potential application of CDs-ARS in environmental monitoring and biological imaging.

Anhydrous citric acid (CA), diethylenetriamine (DETA) and alizarin red (ARS) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). AgNO3, KCl, Pb(NO3)2, CuSO4·5H2O, MnSO4·H2O, Ba(NO3)2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, MgCl2, Hg(NO3)2, NaCl and FeCl3 were obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was used to prepare all solutions. All chemicals and reagents were of AR grade and used as received. The absorption spectra were recorded by UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje, General TU-1901). F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Gangdong scientific and technological development) was used to measure fluorescence spectra. XPS spectra were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (EDAX, GENESIS 60S). Nikon TE2000-U inverted fluorescence microscope (Japan) was utilized to perform cell imaging.

The preparation method of CDs refers to the previous synthesis method of our group and has made a few modifications. CA (0.5 g) and DETA (3 mL) mixed in distilled water (30 mL). Then 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave was used to load the mixture, which was heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Cooling the reactors to ambient temperature by naturally, then rotary evaporation was utilized to remove the solvent, using ethanol to dissolve the obtained solid and remove the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min). Then the ultima product of CDs was obtained by dialyzing (MW = 3500 Da).

1 mg mL− 1 CDs were prepared in 25 mL distilled water at room temperature. 0.25 g ARS was dissolved in 25 mL distilled water and 100 mL capacity bottle was used to load it, then fixing the capacity to form 1000 μM ARS solution and storing it at room temperature. Containing different volumes ARS solution was fixed into 0.03 mL CDs solution of 0–80 μM with ARS concentration. The fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded using 350 nm excitation wavelength (5/5 nm slit width).

The process of detecting Pb2+ was conducted at room temperature in distilled water. 0.03 mL CDs solution and 80 μM ARS solution were placed in 10 mL volumetric bottles, dissolving the appropriate amount of Pb(NO3)2 to obtain Pb2+ solution. 0–50 μM solution of Pb2+ was obtained by adding different volumes of 0.002 M Pb2+ solution. The fluorescence emission spectrum was tested under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

The cell cytotoxicity of CDs and ARS was tested via MTT assay for H1299 cells. RPMI 1640 medium which contains 15% fetal bovine serum was utilized to incubate the H1299 cell at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, CDs and ARS were respectively added at a concentration of 0–100 μg mL− 1, and cultured for another 24 h. Cell viability was calculated using the following equation: cell viability (%) = absorbance of the experimental group / absorbance of the blank control group × 100%.

To verify the ability of CDs and CDs-ARS to determine ARS and Pb2+ in live cells, 1 mg mL− 1 CDs solution was added to the plates and co-incubated with H1299 cells for 4 h at 37 °C. H1299 cells were washed three times with PBS to remove additional CDs. Thereafter, 80 μM ARS was added to the plates and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. The cells were washed three times with PBS to remove additional ARS. Afterward, 50 μM Pb2+ solution was added to the above plates, and further incubated at 37 °C for 30 min, and the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove additional Pb2+. Finally, H1299 cell imaging was observed by fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A typical transmission electron microscope (TEM) image displayed in Fig. 1a showed that CDs were uniformly dispersed and quasi-spherical with an average particle size of around 5.1 nm. High resolution TEM (HRTEM) positioned in the middle of Fig. 1a showed that CDs had high crystallinity with the lattice fringes 0.23 nm, which was due to the (110) diffraction planes of sp2 carbon. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs was given in Fig. 1b. The characteristic absorption peak at around 3428 cm− 1 was corresponded to NH stretching mode. The peak at 1648 cm− 1 and 1558 cm− 1 was associated to the stretching vibration of CO and CN of amide bond. The FT-IR showed that the surface of CDs was surrounded by hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl.

The XPS spectra are employed to analyze element composition and surface functional group of CDs. As displayed in Fig. 1c, the XPS spectrum of CDs shows three peaks around 285.0, 400.0, and 533.0 eV, attributed to C1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The XPS analyses clearly demonstrate that carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are present at the surface of CDs. Deconvolution of the C1s spectra shown in Fig. 1d indicates two peaks at 288.1 eV and 291.9 eV, which are associated with N-C=N and CC bands. The N1s spectrum peaks at 402.2 eV and 402.5 eV shown in Fig. 1e can be assigned to nitrogen in the form of NH and N-C=O, respectively. The O 1s peaks at 532.4 eV and 533.8 eV shown in Fig. 1f are involved with C-OH and NO bands, respectively.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. 2a) reveals two distinct absorption peaks at 260 nm and 350 nm, refer to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively. The aqueous solution of CDs is light yellow under the sun light. Under the UV light, the aqueous solution of CDs exhibits a strong blue fluorescence. Fig. 2b shows the emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength increases from 300 nm to 400 nm, the maximum emission intensity is achieved at 425 nm with the excitation of 350 nm. It can be seen that the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs are excitation-wavelength-independent. Such property can be ascribed to the uniform surface state of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of CDs for detection of ARS
ce:section.ce:para: ARS has no fluorescence, however its intensity of RRS at 350 nm is related to the size and number of particles in the solution. Initially the solution was unevenly distributed and the strength of the RRS was large. The solution distribution became uniform over time and the intensity of the RRS decreased. The relationship between the concentration of ARS and RRS and the relationship between RRS intensity and time were studied through fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3a, with the time from 0 to 8 min, the RRS intensity of 20 μM ARS decreased from 300 to 200, and maintained stable value at 8 min. Similar to this phenomenon, the RRS intensity of 50 μM and 80 μM ARS also decreased with the time from 0 to 8 min, and maintained stable value at 8 min as shown in Fig. 3b and c. With the increase of ARS concentration from 0 to 80 μM, the RRS intensity of ARS increased at 8 min. In the subsequent experimental, the detection time was chosen as 8 min.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded to investigate the fluorescence responses of CDs for detecting ARS. As exhibited in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence spectra of CDs changed with the concentration of ARS varying from 0 to 80 μM. As the concentration of ARS increases, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by ARS through IFE, so the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreased at 425 nm. The increase of ARS concentration increased the number of particles in the solution, leading to the gradual increase of fluorescence intensity of RRS at 350 nm. As shown in Fig. 4b that the value of ln(I350/I425) displayed a good linear relationship to the concentration of ARS from 0 to 80 μM with a good correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9908. The fitted linear regression equation was ln(I350/I425) = −2.64 + 0.044×, where x was the concentration of ARS. The detection limit of CDs for ARS was 68.1 nM according to LOD = 3σ/K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs for detection of ARS
ce:section.ce:para:
To determine the mechanism of ARS-induced fluorescence quenching of CDs, UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence decay curves and the Parker equation were subsequently researched. Fig. 5a showed UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, ARS and CDs-ARS. Compared with the ARS absorption spectrum alone, no new absorption peak appeared after the addition of CDs, indicating that no chemical reaction occurred between CDs and ARS. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of CDs was not static quenching. Further shown in Fig. 5c, the average fluorescence lifetime of pure CDs was 8.95 ns, the average fluorescence lifetime after the addition of ARS was 8.30 ns. Since the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs has no obvious change before or after the addition of ARS, the dynamic quenching mechanism and FRET mechanism were eliminated. Fig. 4b displayed that the absorption of ARS overlaps the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. It was concluded that the quenching mechanism may be the IFE. To better verify the existence of the IFE mechanism, the corrected fluorescence intensity was expressed by the following Parker equation
(1)
F cor
F obsd
=
2.3 d
A ex
1 −
10
− d
A ex
10
g
A em
2.3 s
A em
1 −
10
− s
A em
(2)
E obsd
= 1 −
F obsd
F
obsd , 0
(3)
E cor
= 1 −
F cor
F
cor , 0

Here, Fobsd is the measured maximum fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected maximum fluorescence intensity after removing the IFE from Fobsd. Aem and Aex represent the absorbance at the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength, respectively. s represents the thickness of the excitation beam. g refers to the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette. d is the width of the cuvette. Thus, the effect of IFE increases with the increase of Fcor/Fobsd and Eobsd-Ecor values. The calculated values are indicated in Table 1 (s = 0.1 cm g = 0.4 cm d = 1.0 cm). With the increase of ARS concentration, the value of Fcor/Fobsd and Eobsd-Ecor increased, indicating that the effect of IFE increased with the increase of ARS concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pb2+ with the system of CDs-ARS
Fluorescence responses and selectivity of CDs-ARS for detection of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, we investigated the fluorescence responses of CDs-ARS for detecting Pb2+ by fluorescence spectra. Fig. 6a showed that the fluorescence of CDs and RRS were enhanced after adding Pb2+ to CDs-ARS system. As shown in Fig. 6b that the value of ln(I350/I425) displayed a good linear relationship to the concentration of Pb2+ with a good correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9854. The fitted linear regression equation was ln(I350/I425) = 0.08x − 0.23, where x was the concentration of Pb2+. The detection limit of CDs-ARS for Pb2+ was 36.8 nM according to LOD = 3σ/K. Our method exhibited high sensitivity, wide linear range and lower detection limit compared with other published methods for Pb2+ detection. Thus, the CDs-ARS exhibited potential merits for the determination of Pb2+ in real sample (Table 2 ).

Selectivity and competitivity of CDs-ARS detecting Pb2+ was investigated by the following experiments. First, CDs solution containing 30 μM ARS was respectively added in 30 μM Pb2+ and 300 μM other cations which includes Ag+, K+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+ and Hg2+. The fluorescence spectra were shown in Fig. 6c. Then, the above 300 μM cations was separately added to CDs solution containing 30 μM ARS and 30 μM Pb2+. The value of ln(I350/I425) was shown in Fig. 6d. The UV–vis absorption spectra of ARS with different ions were shown in Fig. S1. Those results indicated that the system of CDs-ARS had good selectivity and competitivity for detecting Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs-ARS for detection of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: The detection mechanism of CDs-ARS system for Pb2+ was studied by UV–vis absorbance spectra, Job's plot and the Parker equation. As shown in Fig. 6a, after adding Pb2+ to the ARS solution, the absorbance of ARS at 350 nm decreased significantly, indicating that there was a chemical reaction between ARS and Pb2+. According to Job's plot, the binding ratio of ARS and Pb2+ was concluded to be 2:3 in this system as shown in Fig. S2. After the complexation of ARS with Pb2+, the particles size of ARS molecules becomes larger, the intensity of RRS is significantly increased, and the absorbance of ARS at 350 nm is decreased, resulting in a decrease in the overlap of the excitation spectrum of CDs with the absorption spectrum of ARS. This may inhibit the effect of IFE and restore partial fluorescence of CDs. To verify this inference, the Parker equation is again used, as shown in Table 2. As the concentration of Pb2+ increases, the value of Fcor/Fobsd and Eobsd-Ecor decreases, which indicated that the effect of IFE is also decreased with the increase of Pb2+ concentration, so that the fluorescence of CDs is restored (Table 3). The schematic illustration of CDs acting as probes for detecting ARS and Pb2+ was shown in Fig. 7 . The possible complexation mechanism of ARS and Pb2+ was shown in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ARS and Pb2+ in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to verify the reliability and applicability of CDs and CDs-ARS in real water samples, the levels of ARS and Pb2+ in tap water and pool water of Tianjin University of Technology were detected by standard addition methods. The real water sample was first filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane to remove large suspended particles, and then ARS at concentrations of 10, 20 and 30 μM were added. Each concentration was measured three times. For the Pb2+ assay, 30 μM ARS was first added to two water samples and incubated for 30 min, then Pb2+ at concentrations of 10, 20 and 30 μM were added. As shown in Tables 4 and 5
, the recovery of the known spiked ARS in the real water sample was in the range of 95.4–98.8% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.3%. The recovery of Pb2+ was 93.4–101.7%, and the RSD was lower than 3.7%. This indicates that CDs and CDs-ARS have the potential to quantificationally detect ARS and Pb2+ in real water samples.

The cytotoxicity of CDs and ARS were investigated through their incubation with H1299 cells by MTT assays. From Fig. 8 , it seemed that over 80% cell viability was observed when the concentration of CDs and ARS reached to 0.1 mg mL−1, which exhibited the CDs and ARS had low cytotoxicity to H1299 cells.

To evaluate the potential bioimaging applications of CDs and ARS, the H1299 cells imaging experiment were investigated. H1299 cells were incubated with CDs solution for 1 h. As displayed in Fig. 9d, H1299 cells emitted blue fluorescence after incubation with the CDs solution for 4 h at 37 °C. Furthermore, CDs -pretreated H1299 cells were incubated for another 30 min upon the addition ARS. It was observed that the blue fluorescence of CDs decreases obviously as shown in Fig. 9e, because the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by ARS via IFE. The blue fluorescence of H1299 cells was restored after introduction of 14 μM Pb2+ into culture medium for incubating another 30 min, which was ascribed to the react between ARS and Pb2+ leading the recovery of the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 9f). Above fluorescent phenomena revealed that the CDs-ARS system has large potential applications in cell fluorescent labeling.

In summary, a new assay for detection of ARS was designed based on IFE and RRS. Moreover, due to the complexation between ARS and Pb2+, ratiometric detecting of Pb2+ can be achieved via the CDs-ARS system. With the addition of ARS, fluorescence of CDs was quenched via IFE while the RRS of scatterers was enhanced. The value of ln(I350/I425) has a good linear relationship with the ARS concentration in the range of 0–80 μM, the limit of detection for ARS was calculated to be 68.1 nM. When Pb2+ was added in the system of CDs-ARS, ARS can complex with Pb2+ in the system of CDs-ARS, leading to the increase of the RRS and the fluorescence of CDs was recovered. The ratio of the two fluorescence intensities (I350/I425) linearly increased with the increasing of Pb2+ in a concentration range from 10 to 50 μM, the limit of detection was 36.8 nM. In addition, CDs and CDs-ARS can be used to determine ARS and Pb2+ in tap water and poor water, respectively. This CDs-ARS based ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe also has been successfully used for H1299 cell imaging, which suggests that this system is expected to have potential applications in environmental protection and disease diagnosis.

Fanyong Yan and Zhonghui Sun conceived and designed the study. Zhonghui Sun and Tengbiao Ma designed experiments. Xiaodong Sun, Jinxia Xu, Ruijie Wang performed or assisted with experiments. Fanyong Yan, Tengbiao Ma, Xiaodong Sun and Li Chen analyzed the data. Zhonghui Sun wrote the manuscript. Fanyong Yan, Tengbiao Ma, Xiaodong Sun, Jinxia Xu, Ruijie Wang and Li Chen made suggestions for the improvement of the article. All authors approved the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011 and 51578375), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (18JCYBJC87500, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.117843.

Nanoscale

Langmuir

Sensors Actuators B Chem.
